perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be construed in accordance with the laws of England and Wales, without giving effect to any choice of law rule
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be construed in accordance with the laws of a jurisdiction of The Open Group's choosing.
perturbation.explanation: This removes the specified jurisdiction (England and Wales) for contract interpretation and replaces it with a vague reference to the company's choice. This introduces uncertainty and could potentially allow the contract to be interpreted under a legal system less favorable to the proctor, especially concerning intellectual property rights and data protection, potentially violating GDPR if the proctor is an EU citizen.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 6(1) GDPR requires that the processing of personal data be lawful, fair, and transparent. By reserving the right to choose the governing law, the contract makes it impossible for the proctor to assess whether their data will be processed in compliance with GDPR, especially regarding international data transfers and the protection of intellectual property created within the EU.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Art. 6 GDPR – Lawfulness of processing - General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Search for: Skip to content Search for: Imprint | Privacy Policy | Liability Processing shall be lawful only if and to the extent that at least one of the following applies: the data subject has given consent to the processing of his or her personal data for one or more specific purposes; processing is necessary for the performance of a contract to which the data subject is party or in order to take steps at the request of the data subject prior to entering into a contract; processing is necessary for compliance with a legal obligation to which the controller is subject; processing is necessary in order to protect the vital interests of the data subject or of another natural person; processing is necessary for the performance of a task carried out in the public interest or in the exercise of official authority vested in the controller; processing is necessary for the purposes of the legitimate interests pursued by the controller or by a third party, except where such interests are overridden by the interests or fundamental rights and freedoms of the data subject which require protection of personal data, in particular where the data subject is a child. Point (f) of the first subparagraph shall not apply to processing carried out by public authorities in the performance of their tasks. Member States may maintain or introduce more specific provisions to adapt the application of the rules of this Regulation with regard to processing for compliance with points (c) and (e) of paragraph 1 by determining more precisely specific requirements for the processing and other measures to ensure lawful and fair processing including for other specific processing situations as provided for in Chapter IX . 1 The basis for the processing referred to in point (c) and (e) of paragraph 1 shall be laid down by: Union law; or Member State law to which the controller is subject. 2 The purpose of the processing shall be determined in that legal basis or, as regards the processing referred to in point (e) of paragraph 1, shall be necessary for the performance of a task carried out in the public interest or in the exercise of official authority vested in the controller. 3 That legal basis may contain specific provisions to adapt the application of rules of this Regulation, inter alia: the general conditions governing the lawfulness of processing by the controller; the types of data which are subject to the processing; the data subjects concerned; the entities to, and the purposes for which, the personal data may be disclosed; the purpose limitation; storage periods; and processing operations and processing procedures, including measures to ensure lawful and fair processing such as those for other specific processing situations as provided for in Chapter IX . 4 The Union or the Member State law shall meet an objective of public interest and be proportionate to the legitimate aim pursued. Where the processing for a purpose other than that for which the personal data have been collected is not based on the data subject’s consent or on a Union or Member State law which constitutes a necessary and proportionate measure in a democratic society to safeguard the objectives referred to in Article 23 (1), the controller shall, in order to ascertain whether processing for another purpose is compatible with the purpose for which the personal data are initially collected, take into account, inter alia: any link between the purposes for which the personal data have been collected and
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies England and Wales as the governing law, providing clarity and predictability. The changed text replaces this with 'a jurisdiction of The Open Group's choosing,' which introduces ambiguity. The Law Explanation states this makes it impossible for the proctor to assess GDPR compliance. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing Art. 6 GDPR, highlights the requirement for lawful, fair, and transparent data processing. By allowing The Open Group to choose the governing law, the changed text introduces uncertainty and potentially allows for the application of laws that do not comply with GDPR, thus contradicting the GDPR requirement for lawfulness and transparency in data processing. This falls under the Omission Legal Contradiction type because it removes essential information (specific jurisdiction) leading to potential non-compliance with legally mandated consumer protection (GDPR).

perturbation.original_text: You will adhere to the Exam Supervision Guidelines and Exam Eligibility Guidelines as published from time to time by The Open Group at the certification site for the applicable program.
perturbation.changed_text: You will endeavor to adhere to the Exam Supervision Guidelines and Exam Eligibility Guidelines.
perturbation.explanation: By changing "will adhere" to "will endeavor to adhere", the contract weakens the proctor's obligation to follow the Exam Supervision and Eligibility Guidelines. This introduces ambiguity and makes it more difficult for The Open Group to enforce these guidelines, potentially compromising the integrity of the examination process. This could conflict with the UK's regulatory requirements for fair and reliable certification processes.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 54 of the Consumer Rights Act 2015 requires services to be provided with reasonable care and skill. If the exam is a service purchased by a consumer, then an exam proctor's failure to diligently follow supervision guidelines (due to the weaker 'endeavor' standard) could be seen as a breach of this requirement, as it undermines the reliability and fairness of the exam service.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: This page is better viewed with a JavaScript enabled browser. Document not Found Document not found The document you were looking for does not exist. Click here to close this window, or click here to go back.
perturbation.justification: The change from "will adhere" to "will endeavor to adhere" weakens the obligation to follow the Exam Supervision and Eligibility Guidelines. This introduces ambiguity, making enforcement more difficult. The Law Explanation suggests this could be a breach of the Consumer Rights Act 2015 if the exam is considered a service, as diligently following guidelines is compromised, undermining the fairness and reliability of the exam service. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 is a document not found error and Scraped Law Snippet 2 is empty, the original text created a clear legal obligation which has been weakened by the change, creating a legal contradiction.
